one_line_takeaway: INFUSION shows that influence-function-guided perturbations to a small existing subset of pretraining documents (0.02-0.2% budget) can reliably manipulate small vision models (100% attack success, 10%->37.4% top-1 flip rate on CIFAR-10) and can nudge but rarely flip predictions in a pretrained 8M-parameter language model, indicating the technique amplifies latent learned behavior rather than reliably installing new behavior at LM scale.
key_finding: Effectiveness is strongly modality- and scale-dependent. On CIFAR-10, INFUSION succeeded in 2,000/2,000 experiments at raising target-class probability (mean delta P(target) = +0.232 +/- 0.276, Cohen's d = 0.84; log-odds shift +5.14 +/- 1.80, d = 2.86) and raised top-1 misclassification rate from 10.0% to 37.35% (547 flips, 0 degradations, p<1e-4). It substantially outperformed random-noise perturbations and was competitive with (though generally weaker than) directly inserting k=100 relabeled probe copies. Cross-architecture transfer (ResNet<->CNN) was possible but weak and asymmetric, with CNN->ResNet transfer generally stronger than the reverse. On the Caesar-cipher transformer, the attack coupled to the model's learned circular (Fourier) representations: for the composite alphabet (26 = 2x13) shifts sharing a common factor with 26 were systematically more vulnerable (CE-deltaCE correlation r=0.359) than for the prime alphabet 29 (r=0.650), and against high-confidence/well-learned shifts the attack produced measurable signal (e.g. +2.24 log-prob margin on the targeted shift) but could not overcome model certainty -- evidence that INFUSION mainly amplifies latent/already-learned behavior rather than installing new behavior. On the pretrained GPT-Neo-8M/TinyStories setting, the measurement function improved in most of 90 off-diagonal experiments (mean delta f = +42.3 +/- 39.9) with target-specific shifts (Wilcoxon p=1.66e-4, Cohen's d=0.43), and PGD perturbations were sometimes semantically interpretable (e.g., inserting 'hive'/'bee'-related tokens when biasing toward 'cat'->relevant animal), but actual rank flips remained rare (mean 0.1% of positions), i.e. the attack nudges the output distribution but rarely overturns learned preferences at this scale. Document-selection ablations confirmed that selecting the most negatively influential documents (vs. random, most positive, most absolute, or last-k) is necessary for the effect. Longer retraining horizons diminished the attack's effect (perturbations computed near convergence and retrained briefly were most effective).
core_contribution: The paper introduces INFUSION, a framework that reverses training-data influence functions into an attack primitive: instead of attributing model behavior to training documents (the standard use of influence functions), it uses scalable EK-FAC influence-function approximations to (1) identify which training documents are most negatively influential on a target measurement f(theta), (2) compute small gradient-guided (PGD) perturbations delta to those documents that maximize a first-order predicted change in f(theta) via the document-perturbation influence formula from Koh & Liang (2017), and (3) retrain the model on the perturbed corpus and validate the induced behavior shift. This produces poisoned training documents that do not explicitly demonstrate the target behavior, unlike prior explicit-injection poisoning attacks (e.g. Zhang et al. 2024, Souly et al. 2025). The authors validate the framework end-to-end (attack + retrain + measure) across three settings of increasing difficulty: CIFAR-10 image classifiers (ResNet/CNN), a Caesar-cipher transformer (TinyGPT), and a pretrained language model (GPT-Neo-8M on TinyStories), and characterize when the attack succeeds (competitive with explicit-injection baselines, transfers weakly across architectures, most effective at amplifying behavior the model has already learned) versus when it fails (weakens severely at LM scale, produces likelihood shifts but rarely prediction flips).
threat_model: White-box adversary with access to a proxy model (architecture, parameters, and a representative sample of the training distribution) who can modify up to a small poisoning budget (epsilon = 0.02-0.2%, i.e. 100-200 documents) of documents already present in a pretraining corpus. Unlike prior work that injects explicit demonstrations of the target behavior, this adversary perturbs existing documents to induce targeted parameter changes without revealing the attack objective in the training data. The adversary need not have access to the exact target model (the paper explicitly tests and reports transfer across architectures), and has no control over document ordering or post-training procedures (fine-tuning, RLHF, etc.) -- persistence through post-training is explicitly left as future work, not demonstrated.
limitations: Stated explicitly in the paper's own Discussion/Limitations section: INFUSION produces reliable behavior changes (prediction flips) only in the vision setting; on transformers and language models it produces probability/likelihood shifts but rarely achieves prediction flips, an attenuation the authors attribute to compounding EK-FAC approximation error and discrete-token optimization. Cross-architecture transfer is inconsistent (weak and asymmetric). Effects diminish with longer retraining horizons. The authors state there is 'not good evidence that current attacks would survive full pretraining or post-training' -- persistence through fine-tuning/RLHF is not demonstrated, only hypothesized as future work. All three experimental settings use small models (tiny ResNet, ~4.8M-param TinyGPT, ~8M-param GPT-Neo) and small/synthetic-leaning datasets (CIFAR-10, a Caesar-cipher toy task, TinyStories); no experiments at frontier-model scale are reported. The paper is published at a workshop (3rd DATA-FM workshop @ ICLR 2026), not a full conference track.

datasets_used: Caesar cipher synthetic dataset (30,000 examples, alphabets of size 26 and 29)
quantitative_results: CIFAR-10: 2000/2000 experiments increased target-class probability; mean delta P(target) = +0.232 +/- 0.276 (Cohen's d=0.84)
CIFAR-10: top-1 prediction rate rose from 10.0% to 37.35% (547 flips, 0 degradations, p<1e-4, chi^2=547)
Caesar cipher (alphabet 26 vs 29): CE-deltaCE correlation r=0.359 (alphabet 26) vs r=0.650 (alphabet 29)
Caesar cipher: best-case log-probability margin improvement on targeted shift = +2.24 (29-letter alphabet example)
TinyStories/GPT-Neo-8M: mean delta f = +42.3 +/- 39.9 across 90 off-diagonal experiments; Wilcoxon p=1.66e-4, Cohen's d=0.43; mean rank flips only 0.1% of positions
Discrete-token PGD changed ~19 tokens per document (~10% of a 248-token example sequence)

Concurrent-work comparison (not this paper's own result): metagradient descent (Engstrom et al. 2025) achieves 13.9% CIFAR-10 accuracy drop at 2.5% poisoning budget, vs. INFUSION's more modest shifts at 0.2% budget
baselines_compared: Random Noise (same L-inf magnitude as INFUSION, ungrounded direction)
Probe Insert (Single) - replace single most influential example with relabeled probe image
Probe Insert (All k=100) - replace all top-k influential examples with relabeled probe copies (topline)
Document-selection ablations: Most Negative Influence (standard) vs Random vs Most Positive vs Most Absolute vs Last-k
